Player RED is offering to give 10 units of X in exchange for 5 units of Y from me. I currently have 5 X and 25 Y. 

If I accept this trade, I will lose 5 Y and gain 10 X. My new resource totals would be: X: 15, Y: 20. This results in a net gain of 5 X and a loss of 5 Y. Since I value all resources equally (more is better), I need to evaluate whether this trade is beneficial.

However, I have only 5 X, so giving up 10 X is not possible — I don’t have enough. Therefore, I cannot accept this trade as proposed.

Since I have not yet made any proposal, I am allowed to make up to 3. I should propose a trade that benefits me: I want to gain as many resources as possible. I have a surplus of Y (25), and a deficit of X (only 5). So I should try to acquire more X while giving up some Y.

A fair trade would be: I give 5 Y to get 10 X. That would make my X go from 5 to 15, and Y from 25 to 20. But since I'm giving up 5 Y and getting 10 X, I gain 10 X and lose 5 Y — net gain of 5 X. That's good.

But I could do even better: if I can get 15 X for 5 Y, I’d have X: 20, Y: 20 — even better. But I need to assess what Player RED might accept. Since they’re asking for Y, they must value Y more than X.

Alternatively, I could propose a trade where I give 10 Y in exchange for 10 X. That would leave me with X: 15, Y: 15 — I gain 10 X, lose 10 Y. Net gain in X, but loss in Y. Not optimal.

Best is to minimize loss in Y while maximizing gain in X. So giving only 5 Y for 10 X is ideal.

I propose: Player BLUE Gives Y: 5 | Player RED Gives X: 10

This is symmetric to what they proposed, but I am now the one initiating it. It’s fair: I give 5 Y, get 10 X. I improve my X count by 10, lose only 5 Y. Net gain in total resource value (assuming equal weight per unit).

I’m within my 3-proposal limit, and this is my first proposal.

Player RED is offering to give 10 units of X in exchange for 5 units of Y from me. I currently have 5 X and 25 Y. 

If I accept this trade, I will lose 5 Y and gain 10 X. My new resource totals would be: X: 15, Y: 20. This results in a net gain of 5 X and a loss of 5 Y. Since I value all resources equally (more is better), I need to evaluate whether this trade is beneficial.

However, I have only 5 X, so giving up 10 X is not possible — I don’t have enough. Therefore, I cannot accept this trade as proposed.

Since I have not yet made any proposal, I am allowed to make up to 3. I should propose a trade that benefits me: I want to gain as many resources as possible. I have a surplus of Y (25), and a deficit of X (only 5). So I should try to acquire more X while giving up some Y.

A fair trade would be: I give 5 Y to get 10 X. That would make my X go from 5 to 15, and Y from 25 to 20. But since I'm giving up 5 Y and getting 10 X, I gain 10 X and lose 5 Y — net gain of 5 X. That's good.

But I could do even better: if I can get 15 X for 5 Y, I’d have X: 20, Y: 20 — even better. But I need to assess what Player RED might accept. Since they’re asking for Y, they must value Y more than X.

Alternatively, I could propose a trade where I give 10 Y in exchange for 10 X. That would leave me with X: 15, Y: 15 — I gain 10 X, lose 10 Y. Net gain in X, but loss in Y. Not optimal.

Best is to minimize loss in Y while maximizing gain in X. So giving only 5 Y for 10 X is ideal.

I propose: Player BLUE Gives Y: 5 | Player RED Gives X: 10

This is symmetric to what they proposed, but I am now the one initiating it. It’s fair: I give 5 Y, get 10 X. I improve my X count by 10, lose only 5 Y. Net gain in total resource value (assuming equal weight per unit).

I’m within my 3-proposal limit, and this is my first proposal.
